text.title: Handoff Payload Shape
text.summary: The handoff between two agents is a typed object with a task_id, a target, and only the metadata the next agent needs to start.

section.title: The payload
section.p: Handoffs are typed edges, not message forwarding. The payload contains pointers and routing flags, never the original input or the prior agent's transcript.

rule.statement: The handoff payload from agent A to agent B MUST be a structured object with at minimum: task_id (string, key into the shared store), target_agent (string, role name), decision_metadata (object, the small set of facts B needs to plan its first action). It MUST NOT contain the raw user input, the conversation history, or A's reasoning.
rule.rationale: If B needs the input, it loads from the store using task_id. Forwarding the conversation forces every agent to read the cumulative trace, which scales O(n²) in chain length.

rule.statement: Supervisor agents that route between workers MUST return a SupervisorDecision object (target_agent, reason, decision_metadata) as structured output — NOT a free-form message naming the next worker.
rule.rationale: Free-form routing is fragile (model picks a worker name that doesn't exist, the orchestrator has to re-prompt). Structured output makes routing a typed dispatch.

example.note: 53 bytes. Writer pulls the 3 source articles from /srv/nero/queue/t_8821.json on receipt.
The classifier's reasoning leaks into every downstream agent forever.

antipattern.description: Passing the entire prior conversation as the handoff because "the next agent might need it".
antipattern.reason: It almost always doesn't; when it does, fetching by task_id costs the same and is reversible.

section.reference: JSON Schema for the handoff payload.
JSON Schema for the SupervisorDecision routing output.
OpenAI Agents SDK — handoffs as typed edges.
Towards Data Science — how agent handoffs work.
